content.problemStatement:
Today is Chef's birthday. His mom gifted him a truly lovable gift, a permutation of first **N** positive integers.

She placed the permutation on a very long table in front of Chef and left it for him to play with it. But as there was a lot of people coming and wishing him. It was interfering with his game which made him very angry and he banged the table very hard due to which **K** numbers from the permutation fell down and went missing.

Seeing her son's gift being spoilt, his mom became very sad. Chef didn't want his mom to be sad as he loves her the most. So to make her happy, he decided to play a game with her with the remaining **N - K** numbers on the table. Chef wants his mom to win all the games.

Chef and his mom play alternatively and optimally. In **Xth** move, a player can choose some numbers out of all the numbers available on the table such that chosen numbers sum up to **X**. After the move, Chosen numbers are placed back on the table.The player who is not able to make a move loses.

Now, Chef has to decide who should move first so that his Mom wins the game.

As Chef is a small child, he needs your help to decide who should move first. Please help him, he has promised to share his birthday cake with you :)
constraints: Value of each of K number belongs to [1,N].
Sum of K over all the test cases does not exceed 5*10^5.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two integers N and K on the first line, followed by K space-separated integers on the second line.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print 'Chef' if Chef should move first, otherwise print 'Mom'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For test case 1, Mom can choose {1} to make 1. Chef can choose {2} to make 2. Mom can choose {1,2} to make 3. Chef can choose {4} to make 4. Mom can choose {1,4} to make 5. Chef can choose {2,4} to make 6. Mom can choose {1,2,4} to make 7. Chef cannot make 8 out of the numbers on the table. So, Chef loses and Mom wins.

For test case 1, Chef can choose {4} to make 4. Mom can choose {5} to make 5. Chef can choose {6} to make 6. Mom can choose {7} to make 7. Chef can choose {8} to make 8. Mom can choose {9} to make 9. Chef can choose {10} to make 10. Mom cannot make 11 out of the numbers on the table. So, Mom loses and Chef wins.

For test case 1, Chef can choose {1} to make 1. Mom can choose {2} to make 2. Chef can choose {3} to make 3. Mom can choose {4} to make 4. Chef can choose {5} to make 5. Mom cannot make 6 out of the numbers on the table. So, Mom loses and Chef wins.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using Nim Theory. The key insight is that the Nim sum of the missing numbers determines who should move first. If the Nim sum is 0, then Mom should move first. Otherwise, Chef should move first.
solution.timeComplexity: O(K) per test case, O(T*K) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) — for storing the missing numbers.
